items.headline: Immune-Evasive DNA Tools Enable Kilobase Editing
items.preview: Researchers have developed INSTALL, a new method for genome editing that reduces immune responses.
items.article:
In a transformative leap for genome editing technologies, researchers have unveiled a breakthrough that surmounts a key obstacle in integrating large DNA sequences into mammalian genomes. The new approach, termed INSTALL, innovatively harnesses mechanisms employed by integrative prokaryotic viruses and mobile genetic elements, demonstrating for the first time that recombinases can successfully work with circular single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) molecules designed to be ‘immune evasive.’ 
Why this matters: This method significantly diminishes toxic innate immune responses in primary human cells, enhancing integration efficiencies and expanding the clinical relevance of recombinase-mediated DNA insertion. The researchers demonstrated INSTALL’s versatility across various system types, suggesting its potential as a universal platform for kilobase-scale genome writing in human cells, which could redefine therapeutic genome editing and synthetic biology applications.

items.headline: A new departure: BioNTech’s founders step aside – Sahin and Türeci in new venture
items.preview: BioNTech's founders, Sahin and Türeci, are stepping down to focus on a new mRNA technology venture.
items.article:
The Mainz-based biotech company BioNTech is facing a historic leadership change as the minds behind the COVID-19 vaccine prepare for a fresh start. Uğur Şahin and Özlem Türeci plan to focus entirely on mRNA platform technology and will launch a spin-off at the end of the year. 
This departure marks a profound turning point for BioNTech, with the supervisory board already searching for successors. The new company will focus on 'next-generation mRNA innovations,' with BioNTech contributing relevant technologies in exchange for a minority stake. Sahin expressed satisfaction with the timing, indicating that BioNTech is now well-positioned for this transition.

items.headline: Roche breast cancer drug falls short of expectations (for now)
items.preview: Roche's giredestrant fails to meet primary endpoint in a key Phase III study, causing stock dip.
items.article:
Roche must report a setback for its experimental oral breast cancer medicine named giredestrant, as a key Phase III study has failed to meet its primary endpoint. While earlier trials had produced encouraging results, the latest study showed that the treatment did not achieve statistically significant improvements in progression-free survival compared to the current standard therapy. 
Despite this disappointment, Roche emphasizes that the clinical development program for giredestrant remains broad, with several large Phase III studies ongoing. Analysts still see potential in the drug, particularly given its earlier successes and the large patient populations that could be treated if further trials prove successful.

items.headline: Allowing Stem Cells Space to Thrive
items.preview: A new method using biodegradable nanogels enhances stem cell therapy for muscle repair.
items.article:
A collaborative research team from Kyoto University and McGill University has developed an innovative approach to stem cell therapy, utilizing biodegradable nanogels integrated into three-dimensional stem cell spheroids. This technique significantly enhances oxygen diffusion and cell viability, addressing a critical hurdle in regenerative medicine for muscle repair. 
The researchers demonstrated that these hybrid spheroids improved muscle tissue regeneration in a rat model of swallowing muscle injury, with increased deposition of new muscle fibers and improved muscle contraction signals. This work exemplifies the integration of bioengineering and stem cell biology to overcome longstanding obstacles in regenerative medicine.

items.headline: Cis-regulatory evolution of Wnt family genes contributes to a morphological difference between silkworm species
items.preview: Research identifies genetic basis for morphological differences in silkworm species.
items.article:
Using interspecific crosses, researchers have identified the genetic basis of the caudal horn size difference between Bombyx mori and Bombyx mandarina. The study reveals that tissue-specific cis-regulatory changes to Wnt1 and Wnt6 contribute to the species difference in caudal horn size. 
This research sheds light on how novel species-specific morphological traits can evolve over relatively short evolutionary timescales, emphasizing the complex genetic architecture underlying morphological evolution. The findings contribute to the understanding of how closely related species can exhibit distinct adaptations.
